document.title: Wireless digital radiography detectors in the emergency area: An efficacious solution

sourceSummary.oneLine: Clinical literature article evaluating implementation of a Carestream Health DRX-1 wireless flat-panel digital radiography system in an emergency radiology area previously using computed radiography.
sourceSummary.officialUse: May support clinical validation and verification evidence for wireless flat-panel digital radiography performance, image quality, radiation dose, workflow, ergonomics, and emergency radiology use.
keyPoints: The study compared CR and WiFi DR systems in an emergency radiology ward.
Image quality improved from 3 to 4.5 on a 5-point radiologist evaluation scale.
Average image acquisition time decreased by 25 seconds per image.
The DRX-1 flat panel showed lower mean radiation in practically all automatic exposimetry techniques, with statistically significant reductions for selected techniques.

evidence.claim: The article is an original report published in Radiología in 2013.
evidence.pageTarget: Wireless Digital Radiography Detectors In The Emergency Area

evidence.claim: The study objective was to evaluate a WiFi flat-panel digital radiography system in an emergency radiology area previously using computed radiography.
evidence.evidenceText: Objectives: To evaluate the implementation of a flat panel digital radiolography (DR) system with WiFi technology in an emergency radiology area in which a computed radiography (CR) system was previously used.

evidence.claim: The installed wireless flat-panel digital radiography system was Carestream Health model DRX-1.
evidence.evidenceText: A flat panel direct digital system with wireless Wi-Fi technology ... was chosen, it was supplied by Carestream Health, model DRX-1

evidence.claim: The DRX-1 system used two flat wireless detectors.
evidence.evidenceText: Carestream Health, model DRX-1, using two flat wireless detectors.

evidence.claim: The DRX-1 system is described as compatible with conventional radiology wards using radiograph chassis or 35 cm by 43 cm CR.
evidence.evidenceText: The DRX-1 system is compatible with any conventional radiology ward using radiograph chassis or 35 cm × 43 cm CR

evidence.claim: The DRX-1 system did not require modifications to the generator or other radiology ward elements.
evidence.evidenceText: it does not require making any modifications either in the generator, or in any other elements in the radiology ward

evidence.claim: The DRX-1 detectors are described as amorphous silicon flat digital detectors with a 2,544 by 3,056 pixel reception area and 139 micrometer by 139 micrometer pixels.
evidence.evidenceText: It consists of one or more amorphous silicon flat digital detectors, with a reception area of 2.544 × 3.056 pixels, each of them 139 µm × 139 µm in size.

evidence.claim: Image quality improved from 3 to 4.5 on a 5-point radiologist evaluation scale after implementation of the DRX-1 system.
evidence.evidenceText: For the CR system, the mean perceived image quality was three points, while with the new DRX1 system it rose to an average punctuation of 4.5 point.

evidence.claim: Average image acquisition time decreased by 25 seconds per image with DRX-1 compared with CR.
evidence.evidenceText: The values of time reduction in image acquisition with DRX1 as opposed to CR showed a mean decrease of 25 s in obtaining every image

evidence.claim: The overall mean radiation level for automatic exposimetry examinations was lower with DRX-1 than with CR, but the global difference was not statistically significant.
evidence.evidenceText: flat panels present a global level of lower mean radiation (1.45 mGy for DRX1; 1.53mGy for CR). However, these global differences are not statistically significant: Z = 0.170; p = 0.43.

evidence.claim: Selected automatic techniques showed statistically significant radiation reductions with DRX-1.
evidence.evidenceText: Table 2 Techniques which presented a statistically significant reduction in radiation in mGy with the new DRX-1 system.

evidence.claim: The article states that patient surface entrance dose values were below reference values established by Spanish legislation and the European Union.
evidence.evidenceText: It can be observed that the SED is, in all the cases, below the reference values that the Spanish legislation and the European Union establish as maximal.

evidence.claim: The authors concluded that installing the wireless flat-panel system in the emergency radiology room was beneficial.
evidence.evidenceText: we conclude that the decision of installing a wireless flat panel system in the radiology emergency room has been beneficial.
